items.q: Did the Cuban Missile Crisis lead to nuclear war?
items.correct: No — both sides backed down: the USSR removed its missiles from Cuba and the USA secretly agreed to remove missiles from Turkey.
items.misconception: The Cuban Missile Crisis ended in a nuclear exchange.
items.random_wrong: The crisis ended with the USA invading the Soviet Union.
items.why: Students dramatise the outcome; the crisis was resolved by negotiation and remains the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war.

items.q: Why was the Berlin Wall built in 1961?
items.correct: East Germany built it to stop the flow of skilled citizens fleeing west through Berlin, which was embarrassing and damaging to the East.
items.misconception: The Berlin Wall was built to keep Western invaders out.
items.random_wrong: The Western Allies built the Berlin Wall.
items.why: Learners reverse its purpose; the Wall was built by the East to keep its own people in, not to repel attack.

items.q: Did the West help the Hungarian Uprising of 1956?
items.correct: No — despite encouraging anti-Soviet feeling, the West did not intervene militarily, and the Red Army crushed the revolt.
items.misconception: The USA sent troops to support the Hungarian rebels.
items.random_wrong: The Hungarian Uprising succeeded in leaving the Soviet bloc.
items.why: Students assume Western action; the West avoided direct conflict with the USSR, leaving Hungary's revolt to be defeated.

items.q: How did the Western Allies defeat the Berlin Blockade of 1948-49?
items.correct: By airlifting supplies into the city
items.why: The answer examiners want is: By airlifting supplies into the city. “By invading East Germany” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was the cause of the Cuban Missile Crisis in 1962?
items.correct: Soviet nuclear missiles placed in Cuba
items.misconception: A US invasion of the USSR
items.random_wrong: The building of the Berlin Wall
items.why: It's Soviet nuclear missiles placed in Cuba. “A US invasion of the USSR” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What happened to the Prague Spring of 1968?
items.correct: It was crushed by a Warsaw Pact invasion
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It was crushed by a Warsaw Pact invasion. “It spread freedom across Europe” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Cuban Missile Crisis occur?
items.why: The real figure is 1962 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
